question: Describe the primary difference between memory-mapped I/O and isolated I/O (port-mapped I/O).
expectedPoints: Memory-mapped I/O uses the same address space for both memory and I/O devices.
Isolated I/O uses a separate, dedicated address space exclusively for I/O devices.
The key distinction is how the processor differentiates an I/O access from a memory access, either by the address used or a dedicated control signal.

question: Explain the process of Program-Controlled I/O (Polling), including its main advantage and a significant drawback.
expectedPoints: The processor repeatedly checks (polls) the status register of an I/O device in a loop to see if it is ready for data transfer.
Its main advantage is its simplicity of implementation in both hardware and software.
A major drawback is that it is inefficient, as the processor is occupied and unavailable for other tasks while waiting for the slow I/O device.

question: Compare and contrast the mechanisms of Programmed I/O (Polling) and Interrupt-Driven I/O. Your answer should detail the sequence of events in an interrupt-driven transfer and explain why this method is generally more efficient for systems with multiple I/O devices.
expectedPoints: In Polling, the CPU proactively and repeatedly checks the device status, wasting cycles. In Interrupt-Driven I/O, the device asynchronously notifies the CPU when it needs service.

Sequence for Interrupt-Driven I/O: 1) CPU issues I/O command and continues other work. 2) Device performs operation. 3) Device asserts interrupt request (IRQ) signal. 4) CPU finishes current instruction, saves state, and jumps to Interrupt Service Routine (ISR). 5) ISR performs data transfer. 6) CPU returns to previous task.

It is more efficient because the CPU is free to execute useful instructions instead of waiting, dramatically improving overall system throughput, especially when managing multiple slow devices concurrently.
